reasoning: Both models performed the initial PKGBUILD audit and independently concluded it is a standard AUR VCS package. The source is the official upstream GitHub repo (git+https://github.com/numixproject/numix-icon-theme.git), SKIP checksums are expected for git sources, and the pkgver() function only runs git describe + sed on the cloned repo. No .install hooks, prepare/build/check functions exist, and package() merely copies icon directories. The package() function was not reviewed because makepkg --nobuild failed to download sources, so both runs were marked 'skipped' rather than 'safe'. The PKGBUILD content itself is benign with no network/filesystem risk beyond the upstream clone. Confidence is medium because the package() function body was not examined by either model, though it is described as a simple icon copy and is low-risk by nature.
disagreement_analysis: No genuine disagreement. Both models agreed the PKGBUILD is safe and both were forced into a 'skipped' status by the same makepkg --nobuild download failure (likely a transient network/clone issue), not by any security concern.
coverage_issues: Neither model reviewed the package() function or full source tree because the source download failed during makepkg --nobuild.
The 'shallow (0 files reviewed)' trigger reflects an infrastructure failure (source fetch) rather than a model deficiency.
learnings: When makepkg --nobuild fails due to source download issues, the tool should distinguish infrastructure failures from genuine audit gaps — a transient clone failure should not be conflated with a 'shallow review' security concern.
For VCS packages where source fetch fails, the tool could fall back to fetching the upstream repo directly (or via a mirror) to allow review of package()/install logic.

The PKGBUILD-only audit was sufficient here to reach a high-confidence benign assessment; the tool could treat icon/theme packages with no install hooks and trivial package() bodies as low-priority for re-audit even when source fetch fails.
Both models' PKGBUILD reasoning was accurate and well-scoped (correctly identified SKIP checksums as normal, pkgver() as safe), indicating prompt quality is good for VCS package recognition.
